narrative.summary:
𝗔𝗥𝗥𝗜𝗩𝗔𝗟

Hicks St in Brooklyn Heights — a tiny bakery that sells out daily.

Afghan baked goods and breads from the team behind Astoria's Little Flower Cafe, with skilled baker Bryan Ford. Opened December 2025. Rating: 4.9 stars.

blocks.title: Arrival
blocks.body:
𝗔𝗥𝗥𝗜𝗩𝗔𝗟

Hicks St, Brooklyn Heights — a tiny storefront that you'd walk right past if you didn't know.

You arrive and the line is already forming. Diljān is the passion project of Ali Zaman and Mohamed Ghiasi (of Little Flower Cafe in Astoria) and baker Bryan Ford — Afghan Americans who wanted to bring the traditional breads and pastries they grew up with to Brooklyn. Open since December 2025, already selling out daily. The name means 'sweetheart' in Farsi.

blocks.title: Space
blocks.body:
𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗦𝗣𝗔𝗖𝗘

Tiny. A counter, a few shelves, the scent of saffron and cardamom. This is a bakery, not a café — you're here to buy and go. The intimacy is part of the charm. You can see Bryan's fingerprints — literally — in every piece of bread.

blocks.title: Food
blocks.body:
𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗕𝗔𝗞𝗘𝗗 𝗚𝗢𝗢𝗗𝗦

𝗣𝗮𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗲𝘀
• Saffron Shah — crescent-shaped, stuffed with saffron pastry cream, dipped in saffron white chocolate. The Islamic crescent symbol. Saffron-forward, the white chocolate breaks it up beautifully ⭐
• Halwa Sticky Bun — sweet roll with semolina pudding (halwa), jaggery, butter, almonds, walnuts, pistachio. Ooey-gooey like a cinnamon bun with Afghan soul ⭐
• Sheer Pira — flaky dough, pistachio frangipane, Afghan milk fudge, vanilla diplomat cream. Dense but light, with cardamom, rose water, sugar syrup ⭐

𝗕𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗱𝘀
• Afghan Naan e Panja — sourdough flatbread with stone-ground flour (Cairnsprings Mills), you can see Bryan's fingerprints in each one. Whole wheat with a crispy bottom ⭐
• Roghani — "oily bread" in Farsi, 18% more olive oil than the panja. Rich, rippable, perfect

Note: Everything sells out. Go early. The saffron shah and the naan are non-negotiable.

blocks.title: Check
blocks.body:
𝗧𝗛𝗘 𝗖𝗛𝗘𝗖𝗞

💰 $

Most items $4-8. Naan under $10.

🌐 @diljanbakery on Instagram

𝗛𝗢𝗨𝗥𝗦
Mon: 8:00 AM – 4:00 PM
Tue–Wed: Closed
Thu–Sun: 8:00 AM – 4:00 PM

𝗕𝗘𝗦𝗧 𝗧𝗜𝗠𝗘𝗦
• Morning opening (8am): Best selection before things sell out
• Thursday/Friday: Fewer crowds than weekends
• Go early or miss the saffron shah — there is no other option

blocks.title: The Vibe
blocks.body:
⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "i am so beyond blown away by the quality and love you can feel in these baked goods. to have an afghan bakery in the neighborhood is an absolutely blessing, and to have one THIS good is a gift. everything here is delicious and feels like a warm hug, i especially love their afghan bread with the scallion cream cheese!"

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "Another excellent addition to Brooklyn Heights. Diljān is an unassuming bakery that punches above its weight. The Sheer Pira was simply delicious and I will definitely be back to try more of their pastries. There is limited seating, a single long bench, so plan to take your pastries and chai to go."

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "Diljan is a tiny hole in the wall offering pastries unlike anything you’ll find elsewhere in NYC (only their sister cafe Little Flower comes close).

The saffron shah was impossibly light and the saffron cream — which was generously portioned inside — was bursting with delicate saffron flavor. I also enjoyed the sheer pira, which was more substantial and had more of a cream cheese moment going on (forgive my ignorance).

The Afghan chai (whole milk only), which I got iced, leaned cardamom forward and was just barely sweet enough, and had wonderful depth of flavor.

The lamb samosa, which is in the form of something akin to a cinnamon roll and topped with powdered sugar (?), was a miss for me, personally. The lamb was a bit dry, and the seasoning and pastry didn’t speak to me.

Unfortunately, I didn’t have a chance to try their bread, which was understandably out of stock on the day of their grand opening. I hope to return to try it (and their accompanying spreads) soon.

The staff were pleasant and helpful. My one gripe was the survey question presented on the payment tablet which asked to choose the best aspect of the experience (service, etc), which I found was unsuited for the pace of business.

The storefront has limited space and even more limited seating — the concept is very ‘grab and go’. I hope they are able to apply for outdoor seating once regulations are loosened under the incoming mayoral admin. The space is aided by its proximity to many picturesque areas (such as the Brooklyn Heights Promenade, the park) to take your pastries and enjoy them when the weather is fair.

I thoroughly enjoyed my experience and will be returning soon. Try this place!"

discovery.text: Eater NY — 'giant naan worth the trip'
